pick.rationale: Wisconsin enters with a substantial analytical advantage, ranking 21-22nd in both Torvik and KenPom compared to High Point's 92-93rd range—a gap of roughly 70 spots that reflects the massive difference between Big Ten and Big South competition. The Badgers boast an elite offense ranked 8th-11th nationally in adjusted offensive efficiency, which should exploit a High Point defense that sits 148th, while Wisconsin's 48th-ranked defense can contain a Panthers offense that hasn't faced a single elite opponent all season (schedule strength ranked 342nd). High Point's 30-4 record and 14-game win streak are impressive, but their gaudy scoring numbers (90 PPG, 4th nationally) are inflated by weak competition, and Wisconsin is motivated after their 2024 first-round loss to James Madison as a 5-seed. The Badgers are also nearing full health with Nolan Winter and Jack Janicki returning to practice, making this a manageable 5/12 matchup despite the historical upset potential.

Subscribe After spending the last few weeks navigating the absence of two key rotational pieces, the 5th-seeded Badgers received one of their most encouraging updates yet from head coach Greg Gard after practice ahead of Wisconsin’s NCAA Tournament opener against 12-seed High Point. Nolan Winter (ankle) and Jack Janicki (wrist), both sidelined with injuries, took a big step forward Monday afternoon. The duo returned to practice after missing time during a stretch in which the Badgers went 6–2 without Janicki and 3–1 in games where both he and Winter were unavailable. “Both of them were in full-go mode, other than contact stuff, major contact stuff. So it’s the most any of them have done,” Gard said . “They both ran full court in some skeleton stuff we did, both were in the drills. Getting Janicki back would also provide another versatile option off the bench — a player whose defensive activity and ability to guard multiple spots have quietly added value throughout the season. Winter, a 7-foot forward, has been one of the most reliable players on the roster this season. He enters the NCAA Tournament averaging 13.3 points, a team-leading 8.6 rebounds per game, while shooting an efficient 56.9% from the field. His production on the glass has been especially important. Winter has recorded 12 double-doubles this season, the second-most in the Big Ten conference, and has reached double-digit rebounds 14 times. Durability had also become part of the Lakeville North product’s identity before the recent absence. MADISON − Wisconsin knows all too well about the dangers of the classic No. 5/No. 12 upset in the Men's NCAA Tournament . Back in 2024, the fifth-seeded Badgers fell in the first round to 12th-seeded James Madison. It was an abrupt end to a 22-win season with major contributions from AJ Storr, Steven Crowl, Tyler Wahl and others. Greg Gard's 2025-26 group will try to avoid repeating history as the current fifth-seeded Badgers will face 12th-seeded High Point in the first round on March 19 in Portland. The Panthers have won their past 14 games en route to earning the automatic bid from the Big South. "I always say, 'Erase the numbers,'" Wisconsin coach Greg Gard said of the seeding. "They're the conference champion in the Big South. They've had a terrific year. Most of their losses were early. ... When we were a one, when we were the two, I wasn't any less nervous." Veteran contributions from Terry Anderson and Rob Martin have been key parts of High Point's 30-win regular season. Anderson and Martin will also be a key test for a Wisconsin defense that had an impressive stretch against Michigan in the Big Ten Tournament semifinals and some woes against inferior foes such as Oregon. Anderson, a 6-foot-6 forward, began his career at the junior-college level before spending two years at Lamar. Now as a graduate transfer at High Point, he leads the Panthers with 16 points per game while shooting 56.4% and averaging six rebounds per game. Martin, a 5-foot-10 guard who transferred from Southeast Missouri, is not far behind Anderson with 15.3 points per game while also shooting 42.2% from the field. He has shown the ability to be a menace on the defensive end, too, as evident when he recorded eight steals in the Panthers' Feb. 4 win over Charleston Southern. The Wisconsin Badgers found out on Selection Sunday that they will be a No. 5 seed in the West Region, taking on the No. 12 High Point Panthers. They will be heading to Portland for the first weekend of the tournament. Heading into Sunday, several Bracketologists projected Wisconsin to earn the last No. 5 seed after a strong week at the Big Ten Tournament, where they beat the Washington Huskies and Illinois Fighting Illini before falling short in a three-point loss to the Michigan Wolverines on Saturday. Wisconsin finished the regular season strong, beating the Washington Huskies (road), Maryland Terrapins, and the Purdue Boilermakers (road), securing another pair of Quad 1 wins. They jumped up to eight with the Illinois win, while their predictive metrics continued to trend upwards across the board into the high 20s. That allowed the Badgers to jump the Tennessee Volunteers, who got a No. 6 seed in the bracket. Wisconsin was ranked as the No. 20 overall seed in the field. Wisconsin will be facing a High Point team that went 30-4 during the season before winning the Big South tournament to clinch a tournament berth. Looking at the rest of the West Region, a potential matchup with the No. 4 Arkansas Razorbacks awaits if the Badgers are able to advance past the first round. result.text: Wisconsin vs High Point prediction, pick, odds March Madness first round Skip to main content Home Sports Packers Brewers Bucks Badgers Golden Eagles Panthers Preps Plus Outdoors Sports Awards Motor Sports Wisconsin basketball vs High Point prediction: Our pick for 2026 NCAA bracket first round John Steppe Milwaukee Journal Sentinel Show Caption Hide Caption An aerial view of the Kohl Center on the campus of UW-Madison An aerial view of the Kohl Center in Madison, Wis., home of the Wisconsin Badgers. MADISON − Wisconsin knows all too well about the dangers of the classic No. 5/No. 12 upset in the Men's NCAA Tournament . Back in 2024, the fifth-seeded Badgers fell in the first round to 12th-seeded James Madison. It was an abrupt end to a 22-win season with major contributions from AJ Storr, Steven Crowl, Tyler Wahl and others. Greg Gard's 2025-26 group will try to avoid repeating history as the current fifth-seeded Badgers will face 12th-seeded High Point in the first round on March 19 in Portland. The Panthers have won their past 14 games en route to earning the automatic bid from the Big South. "I always say, 'Erase the numbers,'" Wisconsin coach Greg Gard said of the seeding. "They're the conference champion in the Big South. They've had a terrific year. Most of their losses were early. ... When we were a one, when we were the two, I wasn't any less nervous." Here are three things to watch in Wisconsin's first-round matchup and our prediction: Buy Wisconsin NCAA tournament tickets vs. High Point How does Wisconsin defend High Point’s veteran duo? Veteran contributions from Terry Anderson and Rob Martin have been key parts of High Point's 30-win regular season. Anderson and Martin will also be a key test for a Wisconsin defense that had an impressive stretch against Michigan in the Big Ten Tournament semifinals and some woes against inferior foes such as Oregon. Anderson, a 6-foot-6 forward, began his career at the junior-college level before spending two years at Lamar. Now as a graduate transfer at High Point, he leads the Panthers with 16 points per game while shooting 56.4% and averaging six rebounds per game. Martin, a 5-foot-10 guard who transferred from Southeast Missouri, is not far behind Anderson with 15.3 points per game while also shooting 42.2% from the field. He has shown the ability to be a menace on the defensive end, too, as evident when he recorded eight steals in the Panthers' Feb. 4 win over Charleston Southern. How does Wisconsin manage pace of play against another up-tempo team? Wisconsin has pushed the tempo to a level that it has not operated at in past seasons in the Bo Ryan and Greg Gard eras. The Badgers' adjusted tempo, as measured by KenPom.com, currently ranks 104th in the NCAA and in the top third of the Big Ten. But for as fast as Wisconsin has operated, High Point has operated even faster. The Panthers' adjusted tempo is 49th in the country, and their average offensive possession length is 24th in the country. UW
